advaita.rendering: The sovereign (prabhuh, the ātman) neither fabricates agenthood (kartrtva) for the world, nor its desired objects — chariots, pots, palaces — nor the union of action with fruit; it is svabhāva (the nature that is māyā, avidyā) that moves. From the ultimate standpoint (paramārthatah), the ātman neither does nor causes anything: what appears as doing is entirely the projection of beginningless nescience. Liberation is the recognition that no real agenthood ever belonged to the self.
advaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Śaṅkara explicitly names svabhāva as avidyā-lakṣaṇā prakṛti (māyā), and lists desired objects (ratha, ghaṭa, prāsāda) as what the ātman does NOT produce — the negation is exhaustive across kartṛtva, karma, and karma-phala-saṃyoga.
advaita.voice_note: Terse, triple-negation dialectic; liberation framed as seeing through the false superimposition.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: The sovereign ātman — abiding in its own intrinsic form (svābhāvika-svarūpa), untouched by karma — does not generate the distinctive agenthood belonging to devas, animals, humans, or plants, nor their specific actions, nor their species-specific fruit-unions. What moves is svabhāva: the residual tendency (vāsanā) built up through beginningless prior karma that has conjoined the ātman with particular forms of prakṛti. Agenthood belongs to conditioned nature, not to the ātman's own essence.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Rāmānuja specifies the full range of beings (deva-tiryaṅ-manuṣya-sthāvara) and makes explicit that the svabhāva here is anādikāla-pravṛtta-pūrva-karma-janita-vāsanā — prior-karma-born residual tendency — not a cosmic illusion but an accumulated relational deposit.
viśiṣṭādvaita.voice_note: Expansive enumeration; agenthood is relational and specific, not abstractly illusory.

dvaita.rendering: The jīva (prabhuh here read as the jīva, lord with respect to inert matter) does not in truth (vastutah) possess agenthood on its own: it is always dependent (jāda-apekṣya, dependent on inert instruments). Agency appears when the jīva misidentifies itself with the body-instrument complex, not as a property of the ātman as such. Hari alone is the ultimate ordainer; the jīva's apparent activity is fully contingent.
dvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Madhva's bhāṣya is brief — 'prabhuḥ hi jīvo jaḍam apekṣya' — identifying prabhu as the jīva and grounding non-agenthood in the jīva's dependence on inert matter. The laconic style is intentional: the verse serves the larger Dvaita axiom of the jīva's radical dependence on Hari.
dvaita.voice_note: Precise, polemical — the prabhuh is NOT Paramātman here but jīva; a deliberate counter-reading to Advaita.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: The Paramātman (prabhu, Īśvara) does not author the agenthood, actions, or fruit-unions of the world of embodied beings — for Īśvara to do so would implicate Him in partiality (vaiṣamya) and cruelty (nairghṛṇya). Rather it is the svabhāva immanent in the world — the nature constituted of manifold womb-seeds and impressions (nānā-yoni-bīja-āśaya) — that moves of itself. The way out is not more karma but surrender of the ego's false agenthood: only then does the ātman's self-mastery (ātmaiva ātmano bandhuḥ) become operative.
śuddhādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Vallabha explicitly invokes BG 6.5 ('ātmaiva ātmano bandhuḥ') as context, names the vaiṣamya-nairghṛṇya problem as the exegetical occasion for the verse, and locates svabhāva in the world-being (loka-niṣṭha), not in Īśvara.
śuddhādvaita.voice_note: Theological precision about Īśvara's innocence; rapturous turn toward the path of ego-dissolution.

bhakti.rendering: The verse answers an Upaniṣadic objection: since śruti says 'He (Paramīśvara) causes the worthy to perform good deeds and the unworthy to perform bad' (Kauṣītakī), is the jīva not simply compelled — and if so, how can it renounce action? Śrīdhara resolves this: Īśvara does not himself generate (na sṛjati) the jīva's agenthood, its actions, or its fruit-unions. Instead Īśvara sets in motion what is already the jīva's own svabhāva — the nature of avidyā and desire accumulated over beginningless time — without becoming its creator. The karmic weight rests entirely with the jīva.
bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: Śrīdhara's bhāṣya is the only one in the panel that opens by quoting the śruti objection in full (Kauṣītakī-style: 'eṣa eva sādhu karma kārayati...'), then uses the verse as a two-verse resolution of the vaiṣamya-nairghṛṇya dilemma. The content is authentic Sanskrit; no HTML artifacts present.
bhakti.voice_note: Dialectical, philological — the verse is read as apologetic theology, not merely metaphysics.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana distinguishes three distinct negations with surgical precision: the ātman (prabhu, the inner sovereign) is neither the inducer of agenthood ('do this' — niyoga-kartā), nor the direct doer of desired objects (ghaṭa, etc.), nor the binder of the doer to fruit (bhojayitā-bhoktā). All three roles are svabhāva's — the divine māyā that appears as avidyā. As a sky does not actually become soiled by the cloud it seems to hold, the ātman is untouched. The upaniṣadic witness-self ('sa samānaḥ san ubhau lokāv anusañcarati... dhyāyatīva lelāyatīva') abides unchanged, luminous beneath every apparent motion.
advaita-bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: Madhusūdana maps three grammatically distinct agenthood-roles (kārayitṛtva / kartṛtva / bhojayitṛtva-bhoktṛtva) onto the verse's three phrases and clinches each with a śruti citation (BU witness-self passage and BG 13.31). The sky-cloud analogy is his own.
advaita-bhakti.voice_note: Synthesizing — Advaita logic applied with bhakti warmth; the witness-self is the devotional ground.

so_what_questions: If no school assigns ultimate agenthood to the ātman, what exactly is held responsible when someone causes harm — and how does each school's answer shape its ethics of accountability?

Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita both invoke svabhāva but mean different things by it (cosmic māyā vs. accumulated karma-vāsanā); does this difference produce different prescriptions for change, or do they converge in practice?
Madhva reads prabhu as the jīva dependent on inert matter, while every other school reads it as Paramātman or ātman-as-sovereign; what is at stake theologically in this single lexical choice?

Śrīdhara is the only commentator to front-load an Upaniṣadic objection before resolving it; what does this dialogical structure reveal about how the bhakti-philological tradition reads scripture — as settled doctrine or live debate?

All six schools guard against the vaiṣamya-nairghṛṇya (divine partiality/cruelty) problem; does the fact that they converge on the same worry but diverge on the solution suggest the verse was deliberately composed as a multi-interpretable safeguard?

Vallabha alone explicitly cites BG 6.5 as interpretive context for 5.14; does reading the two verses together change what 'svabhāva pravar­tate' means — and what does this intertextual choice reveal about Puṣṭi-mārga hermeneutics?

Madhusūdana's sky-cloud analogy is exegetically generated, not in the mūla; at what point does a commentator's analogy become authoritative tradition — and how does this verse's reception history illustrate that process?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself rehearsing 'I did this, I caused that,' treat it as the smoke of avidyā (unknowing), not testimony. Practice tracing credit and blame back to the conditioned stream — body, habit, circumstance — without landing the weight on a fixed self. The goal is not apathy but the lightness that comes when the self stops defending a ledger it never actually owned.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Recognize that your characteristic style of acting — the way you instinctively lead, avoid, or help — is not 'you' but an accumulated vāsanā-pattern laid down by prior relationships, roles, and karmic encounter. This is not fatalism: the pattern is yours to offer as kainkarya (service) to Bhagavān rather than to hoard as personal identity. Redirect the habitual energy; do not merely suppress it.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any moment of 'I am the one making this happen,' look for what inert structure you are leaning on — the body, the tool, the institution, the social role. Your agency is real but always borrowed and dependent. Cultivate the humility of a vessel: act fully, claim nothing, refer all effective power to Hari.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Stop auditing your own motives for purity before acting. The ego that watches itself act, judging whether it is being selfless enough, is itself the ahaṃkāra (ego-sense) that the verse is dissolving. Offer the action to Kṛṣṇa before, not after: the offering changes the action's metabolism, not its external form.
everyday_applications.bhakti: When you feel compelled by habit, appetite, or social pressure to act against your better judgment, do not immediately blame yourself or Providence. The verse names this precisely: svabhāva — your own accumulated nature — is what moves you. The task is not to erase svabhāva in one willful stroke but to bring it consciously into relationship with discernment (viveka), gradually loosening its autonomous hold.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Distinguish three distinct moments of ownership you attach to action: 'I directed this' (inducer), 'I produced this' (maker), 'I deserve this result' (enjoyer). Drop each separately. You need not wait until all three dissolve at once — even loosening one creates interior space. The śruti promise is that the witness-self, the ātman that 'seems to meditate, seems to tremble' (dhyāyatīva lelāyatīva), is already free; practice is simply stopping the re-narration.

self_assessment: Panel grounded in all six bhāṣyas with real divergence preserved; Madhva bhāṣya brevity acknowledged; Śrīdhara content confirmed free of HTML artifacts; vaiṣamya-nairghṛṇya thread traced across schools; schema conforms to spec.
